summary: Fernando Pérez traces his unconventional path from theoretical physics to creating IPython and co-founding Jupyter, arguing that open source scientific computing tools are far more than software — they are infrastructure requiring community governance, professional management, and sustainable funding. He demonstrates Jupyter's impact through examples ranging from Berkeley's massive data science courses to Nobel Prize-winning gravitational wave research, while highlighting the crisis that builders of foundational open source tools face in academia. Pérez makes a compelling economic case that investing just 0.1% of the federal R&D budget ($200 million) in open source infrastructure would yield enormous returns, but emphasizes the hard organizational problem of spending that money well. He frames the challenge as fundamentally about people, incentives, and governance — not code.
key_points: Jupyter is not just software but an ecosystem of protocols, standards, services, content, and community — with community management being the hardest part
Foundational open source scientific tools exist largely in spite of academic incentive structures, with key creators like John Hunter, Travis Oliphant, and William Stein forced out of academia

The federal R&D budget is ~$200 billion, and investing just 0.1% ($200 million) in open source infrastructure would yield massive ROI while mitigating the risk created by the shift away from commercial tools
Recognizing open source work as professional, paid work is itself an act of inclusion — relying on volunteer labor excludes anyone who cannot afford to donate their nights and weekends
Projects like Pangeo succeed by doing unglamorous last-mile integration work rather than building new things from scratch, but this is exactly the kind of work that doesn't get funded or rewarded
money_quotes: It was born as a toy little Python script at the beginning. That's what I called my advisor. I wanna play with this Python thing for an afternoon, I'll be back to work tomorrow. It's been 20 years and I never came back.

All of this stuff, which now powers all of research, exists pretty much in spite of the system. Not just not thanks to it, but almost in spite of the support of the funding infrastructure and the academic incentive system.
If you say, oh, volunteers can do this job, nights and weekend people can do this job, you're saying, well, people who can afford to volunteer can do this job.
We end up with not two, but 10 highways, half built, all of which take us to the same half spot in the middle of nowhere, and not a single one that actually takes us to where we need to go.
The paper is the glossy sales brochure. It's like a little glossy. But you need to actually give people the inspections book.
I remember the very first time we had a conversation with IBM. We said, oh yeah, let us show you the link to our governance documents. You could hear the relief in their voices.
The open source community is not something like magical pixie dust of people who eat air and live in the clouds and produce code. There are people like you and me who still need a job.
Recognizing that this can and should be a job which is allocated, which is recognized, and where people are included is actually inclusive, and we should do it.
key_insight: Open source scientific infrastructure is a governance and economics problem masquerading as a software problem, and treating it as professional work worthy of sustained investment is both pragmatically necessary and ethically imperative.
